blocks.text: Psalm 60
Deliver me from my enemies, O my God; protect me from those who rise up against me.

Ps 59: Prayer against the enemies of the community. The designation of the enemies as “all the nations” (vv. 5,8), “the city” (vv. 6,14), and “known to the ends of the earth” (v. 13) suggests that the poem is a petition of the community, perhaps uttered by the king, the “I” voice in this psalm. The doubled structure of the poem (vv. 1–10,11–17; note the refrains in vv. 6,14 and 9,17) enables the sufferer to change from a simple desire for personal rescue to a passion for justice, hope, and eagerness to sing praise.
Deliver me from those who work evil; from the bloodthirsty save me.
Even now they lie in wait for my life; the mighty stir up strife against me. For no transgression or sin of mine, O Lord,
for no fault of mine, they run and make ready. Rouse yourself, come to my help and see!
4 : , see Ps 44.23n.
You, Lord God of hosts, are God of Israel. Awake to punish all the nations; spare none of those who treacherously plot evil. Selah
5 : (also v. 13), see Ps 3.2n.
Each evening they come back, howling like dogs and prowling about the city.
There they are, bellowing with their mouths, with sharp words on their lips— for “Who,” they think, “will hear us?”
6–7 (cf. 14–15): The enemies are like dogs, not attacking but constantly yelping, and their cry is i.e., God is unable to save the city.
But you laugh at them, O Lord; you hold all the nations in derision.

8 : introduces the statement of praise in vv. 8–10 just as (v. 16) introduces the statement of praise in vv. 16–17. The words are similar to Ps 2.4, in a similar context of God mocking the nations for believing that he is powerless to save Israel.
O my strength, I will watch for you; for you, O God, are my fortress.
My God in his steadfast love will meet me; my God will let me look in triumph on my enemies.
Do not kill them, or my people may forget; make them totter by your power, and bring them down, O Lord, our shield.
For the sin of their mouths, the words of their lips, let them be trapped in their pride. For the cursing and lies that they utter,
consume them in wrath; consume them until they are no more. Then it will be known to the ends of the earth that God rules over Jacob. Selah
11–13 : The psalmist wants God to make an object lesson out of the enemies.
Each evening they come back, howling like dogs and prowling about the city.
They roam about for food, and growl if they do not get their fill.
But I will sing of your might; I will sing aloud of your steadfast love in the morning. For you have been a fortress for me and a refuge in the day of my distress.
16 : , see Ps 57.9–10. complements the military imagery that predominates.
O my strength, I will sing praises to you, for you, O God, are my fortress, the God who shows me steadfast love.
16–17 : As is appropriate to this psalm, God is depicted metaphorically as , and ; see Ps 18.1–2.
